codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: self-review substituted — 5 adversarial questions generated and applied before finalizing report

app_understanding.note: No running web application; this is a code/configuration architecture review of the .claude command system

console_errors_checked.note: Not applicable — no web app; .claude is a command/hook system, not a browser app

network_requests_analyzed.note: Not applicable — no network layer in scope

performance_benchmarked.note: Not applicable — no browser performance in scope

issues.title: /close normal-path preflight hard-blocks when /do was used instead of /dev
issues.description: commands/close.md lines 159-165 define a hard preflight check that runs BEFORE inspector dispatch (Step 1). It requires ALL FIVE of: docs/dev/ticket-<task-id>.md, docs/dev/context-<task-id>.json (with nested request_id and task_id fields), docs/dev/dev-report-<task-id>.json (with nested dev.status=="completed" plus dev.files_modified and dev.files_created arrays), docs/dev/qa-report-<task-id>.json (with nested qa.status=="pass"), and docs/dev/completion-<task-id>.md. Every one of these five artifacts is produced exclusively by the /dev subagent chain (BA at Step 3, dev at Step 8, QA at Step 11, orchestrator at Step 15). /do is a hook-only command (commands/do.md line 6: "hook-only command; body is not injected") whose sole effect is writing a consent flag at /tmp/claude-orchestrator-consent-<session-id>.flag. /do produces zero of the five required artifacts. Any /close invocation after a /do-only development session therefore fails at the preflight gate — before any inspector runs — with exact path and reason reported for each missing artifact.
issues.observation_notes: The artifact dependency chain is asymmetric: /close was designed assuming /dev is always the predecessor and encodes that assumption as a hard precondition. /do was designed as a bypass for the orchestrator gate, not as a development workflow. The two roles do not intersect in artifact production.

issues.title: Task-id resolution in /close fails silently when no /dev session context exists
issues.description: commands/close.md lines 142-150 define three task-id resolution paths: (1) explicit path argument, (2) explicit timestamp argument verifying both ticket-<ts>.md and qa-report-<ts>.json exist, (3) no argument — requires the orchestrator to have run /dev in the same session. When /do is used, path (3) produces: 'No spec identified. Either run /close within a conversation that just completed /dev, or provide an explicit path/timestamp.' Path (2) still fails at preflight (arch-1) even if the timestamp is supplied, because qa-report-<task-id>.json was never created. There is no resolution path for the /do scenario that bypasses both the task-id resolution failure and the preflight artifact check simultaneously, except /close --force.
issues.observation_notes: The task-id resolution and the artifact preflight are sequential gates; failing the first prevents the second from even being evaluated. This means the error message a user sees (task-id resolution failure) may not accurately indicate the deeper problem (missing artifact chain).

issues.title: /close --force is an incomplete workaround: resolves /close gate but /commit downstream remains partially blocked
issues.description: commands/close.md lines 53-139 document the --force path, which explicitly 'short-circuits before this normal-path preflight' (line 165) and writes a forced close-report. This makes /close itself succeed. However, /commit (commands/commit.md) dispatches changelog-analyst, which performs 'dev-report enrichment' (agents/changelog-analyst.md lines 111-129). Crucially, changelog-analyst.md line 98 states 'untracked entries do NOT require a matching dev-report entry. The dev-report is attribution enrichment only.' and line 129: 'These paths are used for commit message enrichment only. They do NOT add files to the commit that are not already in git status output.' This means /commit CAN succeed after /close --force without a dev-report — it will commit all git-status-dirty files but produce a less informative commit message (no type/scope enrichment from the dev-report). The user's immediate close-and-commit goal is achievable via /close --force followed by /commit --force, but the commit message will be degraded.
issues.observation_notes: The /close --force path was designed for the 'Codex hits quota limit / Workflow Integrity known-acceptable artifact' escape hatch (close.md line 126-129), not for the 'no /dev artifacts at all' scenario. The forced close-report template (lines 67-95) explicitly acknowledges 'absence of upstream artifacts becomes visible in the report and is intentional' (line 97), but this documentation is inside the --force section and not surfaced to the user encountering the preflight failure.

issues.title: Workflow Integrity Dimension Bullet 2 (task-id chain consistency) will always FAIL for /do cycles
issues.description: commands/close.md lines 236-240 define Workflow Integrity Dimension Bullet 2: 'Are predecessor artifacts (BA spec → context → dev-report → completion → qa-report → close-report) ALL present under the SAME <task-id>?' For any /do-based development, this chain has no members (no BA spec, no context, no dev-report, no completion, no QA report). If /close somehow reached QA evaluation (which it cannot on the normal path due to arch-1), Bullet 2 would evaluate to FAIL, forcing CLOSE: NO regardless of QA/codex positions per verdict branch 4 (line 317: 'any of the four bullets evaluates to FAIL → CLOSE: NO regardless of QA / codex positions'). This is a second layer of blocking even if the preflight were bypassed.
issues.observation_notes: The Workflow Integrity Dimension was designed to enforce the /dev artifact chain as a mandatory precondition for closure. The --force path bypasses ALL four bullets (line 88: 'Workflow Integrity Dimension: ALL bullets OVERRIDDEN — not evaluated'), so --force is the only path through this second blocking layer.

issues.title: No documented escape hatch for the 'simple /do development that needs closure' pattern
issues.description: The commands/close.md documentation describes --force as an escape hatch for three specific scenarios (line 126-129): Codex quota exhaustion, Workflow Integrity bullet flagging a known-acceptable artifact, and operator accepting risk after reading all dissent. The scenario 'development done via /do with no /dev artifacts' is not listed as a --force use case. The warning 'strongly discouraged for routine use' (line 126) may discourage users from discovering that --force is actually the correct tool here. There is no documentation bridge between 'I used /do to develop something simple' and 'use /close --force to close it'.
issues.location: commands/close.md:125-130 (escape hatch documentation); commands/do.md (no mention of /close incompatibility)
issues.observation_notes: The absence of this escape hatch documentation creates a dead end for users who correctly use /do for simple changes but then cannot close the work. The user's reported experience ('cannot be closed') maps precisely to this documentation gap.

artifact_contract_map.description: Exact artifacts /close requires vs. what /do produces

required_by_close_normal_path.validation: nested dev.status=="completed", nested dev.files_modified array, nested dev.files_created array; top-level-only status fields rejected
nested qa.status=="pass"; top-level status or verdict fields rejected

produced_by_do.note: Session-scoped, auto-clears at stop. Not a /close artifact.

close_force_bypass.downstream_commit_impact: changelog-analyst treats dev-report as attribution enrichment only (not authority); /commit can succeed without dev-report but commit message type/scope enrichment is unavailable

summary.root_cause: commands/close.md's normal-path preflight (lines 159-165) encodes a hard dependency on the complete /dev artifact chain (5 artifacts from BA, dev, QA subagents, and orchestrator). commands/do.md is a consent-flag-only command that produces none of these artifacts. The design assumption that /dev always precedes /close is not documented as a constraint in commands/do.md, creating a workflow dead-end when /do is used for simple development.
blocking_sequence: 1. /close invoked with no args → no session /dev context → task-id resolution fails with 'No spec identified' error (close.md:148-150)
2. /close <timestamp> invoked → task-id resolved → preflight checks ticket-<ts>.md (missing) → blocks before Step 1 (close.md:163-165)
3. Even if preflight bypassed: QA Workflow Integrity Bullet 2 evaluates ALL predecessor artifacts missing → verdict branch 4 forces CLOSE: NO (close.md:317)
4. /close --force → bypasses all above → writes close-report-<task-id>.md with CLOSE: YES (FORCED)
5. /commit after --force → changelog-analyst uses git status as authoritative file set; dev-report is enrichment-only → commit succeeds with degraded message attribution
clean_areas: --force escape hatch correctly bypasses both the preflight and all Workflow Integrity bullets
changelog-analyst.md correctly treats dev-report as attribution-only, not authority — /commit does not hard-require dev-report
The artifact contract is internally consistent for the /dev-first workflow it was designed for
The forced close-report template explicitly acknowledges artifact absence and records it in the audit trail
ba_investigation_recommendations: Determine whether /close should gain a '--no-dev-artifacts' mode that skips the preflight but still runs inspector evaluation on the actual git diff
Determine whether commands/do.md should document the /close --force path as the required closure method when /do is used for development
Determine whether the Workflow Integrity Dimension Bullet 2 should have a waivable N/A path for /do-origin cycles (similar to how Bullet 4(iv) has N/A-with-reason for user-only filesystem actions)
Determine whether a minimal stub artifact set could be auto-generated for /do cycles so the normal /close path becomes viable
